title: Enami-Rio Tinto’s Altoandinos Salt Flats Project holds 25% of Chile’s total lithium
Norwegian police detain three over attack on US embassy
content: The powerful early morning ‌blast on Sunday from an improvised explosive device (IED) damaged the entrance to the embassy's consular section but caused no injuries, Norwegian authorities have said. The three suspects, all in their 20s, are Norwegian citizens with a family background from Iraq, police said. "They are suspected of a ‌terror bombing," Police ‌Attorney Christian Hatlo ⁠told reporters. "We believe they detonated a powerful bomb ​at the US embassy with the intention of taking lives or causing significant damage," Hatlo said, adding that none of the suspects had so far been interrogated. Reuters could not immediately establish whether the three admitted to any crimes or maintained their innocence. Images of one ⁠of the suspects released by police on ‌Monday showed ​a hooded person, whose face was not visible, wearing dark clothes and carrying ​a bag or ‌rucksack. Investigators on Monday said one hypothesis was that the incident was "an act of ​terrorism" linked to the war in the Middle East but that other possible motives were also being explored. Police are investigating whether the bombing was ​done ​on behalf of another country although other possible motives were also being ‌explored, Hatlo said. European countries has been on alert for possible attacks as the US and Israel conduct air strikes on Iran and Iran launches drone and missile attacks on Israel and US targets in the Middle East. On Monday, a synagogue in the Belgian ​city of Liege was damaged by a blast that authorities called an ​anti-Semitic attack. It was not ⁠clear who was behind it.

title: Er, wrong St James' Park ... fan's big match mistake
content: Having planned to watch Barcelona's last-16 first-leg clash against the Magpies on Tuesday at St James' Park, the supporter found himself instead at St James Park in Devon, home of Exeter City, before a League One clash against Lincoln City. The unfortunate fan had miscalculated by just under 600 kilometres -- or a six-and-a-half hour road trip. Adam Spencer, Exeter's supporter experience officer, explained: "One of our volunteers came to the office to let us know that this guy had turned up expecting to see FC Barcelona. "His English wasn't great, but from what we could gather, he'd come from London. My guess is he'd put St James Park in his phone and then just followed the directions from there. "He was pretty gutted and a bit embarrassed. So, we sorted him out a ticket and he got to watch a game at the real St James Park! He'd be welcome back any time." Alas, Exeter lost their home match 1-0.
creator: by                    aap newswire

Observer-Reporter Athlete of the Week
content: Name: Marlee Solomon Grade: Junior School: Canon-McMillan Sport: Wrestling Solomon’s week: An exclamation point was put on an amazing season by Solomon by dominating all four of her matches at the PIAA girls wrestling tournament en route to her second consecutive state title. “I’m just really grateful, because not everyone gets the opportunity to win two (state) titles, so I’m really proud of that,” Solomon said. “I’m really happy that I did it and most importantly had fun doing it as well.” Solomon won by tech fall in each match, including a 16-1 win in the second period of the 118-pound championship match over Olivia Kearns of Shippensburg. “I faced her in the finals at Powerade and that was a tough match,” Solomon said. “She’s a really hard-working opponent that’s really talented. I’m glad I got to wrestle her again, because I learned a lot. I’m happy with how I performed. Never Satisfied, but proud of what I was able to do.” A season to remember: Solomon collected plenty of gold medals and finished off a perfect 39-0 record, improving to 79-3 overall in two years since moving from California to Pennsylvania. She won the Beast of the East, Powerade and Women of Ironman titles to secure the trifecta of major in-season tournaments. She also won at the Butler Tournament, her second WPIAL title and second regional championship. She said she still really hasn’t processed what she’s accomplished, but can pinpoint the reason why she had so much success. She attributed it to a change in mindset. “I used to get so stressed about winning and losing and if I’d lose it get really down,” Solomon said. “That was my mindset going into every match and I wouldn’t perform as well as I could, because I was so worried about the outcome. This year I was mostly focusing on having fun and just letting it fly. I told myself that there was no point in doing wrestling if I didn’t enjoy it. The shift in mindset definitely allowed me to have more fun this year. It’s something I’m proud of that I was able to get out of that headspace.” Aiming for Worlds: Solomon’s next goal is to represent the United States on the national junior girls wrestling team. She is headed to Spokane, Washington in early April to compete at the USA Wrestling Women’s National Championships. If Solmon wins her weight class, then she’ll make the national girls wrestling team and compete at Worlds this summer. She’s going to compete for a spot on the U20 and U17 teams. “That’s been my main goal ever since I...

City of Olean to seek lead water line, sewer loans from state
content: The bypass site at the South Fourth Street lift station on the Allegheny River in Olean has been the source of multiple wastewater discharges. OLEAN -- City officials hope to receive state aid for sewer and water line upgrades. The Olean Common Council voted Tuesday to authorize an application to the Clean Water State Revolving Loan Fund for work on the sanitary sewer collection system, as well as an application to the Drinking Water State Revolving Loan Fund for the city’s lead service line inventory and replacement program. City Department of Public Works Director James Sprague said the sanitary sewer application is tied directly to a state Department of Environmental Conservation consent order -- an agreement to make investments in the sanitary sewer system to avoid discharges of untreated wastewater into the Allegheny River watershed. “It's basically a low-interest loan,” he said, but noted that based on scoring for the municipality, “the state administrators can choose to forgive some of the principal once you've completed the project.” The state is managing and funding an engineering report on the system, which has not yet been completed, Sprague said, so he could not tell the council exactly what work would be performed yet. “But right out of the box, we would direct any loan application at any actions we have to take under the consent order,” he said. The city first entered into two consent orders in the 2000 for wastewater discharges, with a focus on the wastewater treatment plant and a commitment to spent at least $200,000 a year on sewer line improvements. Following several high-profile discharges in 2024, the city and state agreed to an updated consent order in September, including $20,000 in immediate and deferred penalties. Some of those stipulations include creating an engineering report regarding inflow and infiltration sources, actionable projects to eliminate the inflow and infiltration, and schedule out the projects, shut off all municipal stormwater system entries into the sanitary sewer system, and meet various reporting requirements. The state announced the planning effort in June, setting aside $500,000 for the work. Council President Vernon Robinson Jr., I-Ward 6, thanked Sprague for working on the applications. “The council is definitely happy with this,” he said, noting the council had asked the city administration to find alternate funding opportunities for the effort. The second application aims to identify lead service lines...

Pitt drops high-scoring ACC Tournament game
content: CHARLOTTE, N.C. (AP) — Quadir Copeland had 24 points and eight assists, Ven-Allen Lubin added 18 points and No. 7 seed N.C. State beat 15th-seeded Pittsburgh 98-88 on Wednesday in the second round of the ACC Tournament. N.C. State (20-12) advances to play No. 2 seed and 10th-ranked Virginia in the quarterfinals on Thursday. N.C. State led 51-50 at halftime as both teams shot over 60% from the field and had three players in double-figure scoring. The Wolfpack went 7 of 9 from 3-point range, while Pitt was 9 of 16. N.C. State began the second half on a 12-2 run to extend its lead to 63-52 before Pitt made its first field goal at the 16:31 mark. Matt Able made the Wolfpack’s 11th 3-pointer — in just 15 attempts — for a 76-62 lead. The officials upgraded to a flagrant-1 foul with 2:13 remaining after Lubin was pushed from behind on an alley-oop attempt. Lubin made both free throws to give N.C. State a 92-83 lead and Paul McNeil Jr. made it a four-point possession for an 11-point lead on an easy layup. McNeil finished with 15 points, Tre Holloman had 14, and Darrion Williams and Able each scored 12 for N.C. State. Cameron Corhen scored a career-high 27 points for Pittsburgh (13-20). Nojus Indrusaitis added a season-high 19 points and Omari Witherspoon also scored 19. Pitt reached its season average for made 3-pointers in a game with 8:40 left in the first half when Indrusaitis made his third straight and the Panthers’ eighth in 12 attempts for a 34-25 lead. The Panthers finished 12 of 27 behind the arc, while the Wolfpack went 13 of 23. Up next N.C. State lost both regular-season matchups with Virginia this season, most recently a 90-61 contest on Feb. 24.

GOACC’s March Business After Hours at The Hatch Café
content: OLEAN — Attendees are in for a treat this month at the Greater Olean Area Chamber of Commerce’s March Business After Hours at The Hatch Café. The Hatch is located at 260 N. Union St. on the Jamestown Community College campus; BAH is set for 5 to 7 p.m. March 18. The Hatch team is excited to welcome GOACC members and prospective new members and share the story behind the hometown campus café. Come for light refreshments and samples of their delicious baked goods, tours of JCC and remarks from Kathleen Martel, director of workforce development, and Brianna Stayer, coordinator of innovation and entrepreneurship, highlighting the exciting programs and opportunities on campus. The group will be joined by Attendance Fund Sponsor, Armstrong; and Special Guest, Olean’s Wellness Store, will share its latest products to help you feel better as we transition from winter to spring. Stop by registration on your way in. There will be a 50/50 raffle and the chance to enter the drawing for the Attendance Award. At the end of our 2026 BAH season, one name will be drawn from the list of attendees, and they will win the Attendance Fund Sponsors’ jackpot. Business After Hours is an engaging and fun networking event where you can visit and connect with other area businesses, public officials, and members of the community. To RSVP to attend this month’s BAH, email events@OleanNY.com or call (716) 372-4433. If you cannot make this month’s Business After Hours, mark your calendar for next month’s, April 15, at The Challenger Learning Center, with Attendance Fund Sponsor InfoSecurity Blueprint. LUNCH & LEARN SET FOR MARCH 23 Ready to move your business onward and upward? Join the Greater Olean Area Chamber of Commerce’s Virtual Lunch & Learn series this month for a presentation from Pursuit, a Buffalo-based business that provides businesses with small business loans and resources. Ethan Ruot will present “Access to Capital for Your Small Business” at noon March 23, touching on different loan programs. Participants can sign up for Lunch & Learn online, email Info@OleanNY.com or call (716) 372-4433.

Walmart's bestselling $30 tote bag is on sale for just $9, and it comes in 21 colors
content: TheStreet aims to feature only the best products and services. If you buy something via one of our links, we may earn a commission. Why we love this deal As much as I love a canvas tote bag , some are super flimsy and prone to breaking and ripping, which is the last thing you want in a bag that you plan on using all the time. I use totes for everything from groceries to estate sales, so durability is high up on the must-have list, with style at a close second. I spotted a corduroy tote bag at Walmart that looks durable and sturdy with a double lining and cool, trendy look, and it's on sale now for as low as $9. During a Walmart Flash deal, the Ayieyill Corduroy Tote Bag is on sale for just $9 — that's 70% off its regular price of $30. Available in 21 colors, you can choose solid hues, spring-ready florals, and playful patterns that cater to minimalists, maximalists, and everyone in between. Ayieyill Corduroy Tote Bag, From $9 (was $30) at Walmart Courtesy of Why do shoppers love it? Canvas tote bags are universally loved, but a corduroy tote bag? Now, that's how you elevate a classic. The tote bag's corduroy fabric has a soft and stylish texture that takes a simple accessory up a notch without complicating its design. Some canvas totes can be on the flimsy side, but the corduroy makes it extra durable, and there's cotton lining that makes it even more sturdy. With a spacious design that measures 14.5 inches long by 4.3 inches wide by 13.5 inches high, you'd be surprised by how much you can fit in this tote bag. Shoppers say it's the "perfect size," whether you're using it to carry a laptop to school or work, travel essentials for a weekend getaway, or crafts, reading materials, and other screen-free activities as an affordable analog bag. It even has two slip pockets to store smaller items, like keys, a phone, and lip balm. The tote also has snap button closures to keep your belongings secure, which is a feature a lot of other tote bags in this price range don't have. Last but not least, the tote bag is available in 21 colors, with sale prices ranging from $9 to $10. There are so many patterns to choose from, including a classic solid black color, colorful or retro florals, checkered print, animal prints, and more. At just $9 each, you can get a few without breaking the bank. title: Tickets are now on sale for revived Festival of Nations
content: Tickets are on sale for the revived Festival of Nations, which is now known as the International Festival of Minnesota. Guiee Aphirachataom, a traditional Thai Dancer, explores the Festival of Nations at RiverCentre in St. Paul on Thursday, May 3, 2018. Tickets are on sale now for the revived Festival of Nations, now known as the International Festival of Minnesota. (John Autey / Pioneer Press) The event — which features global cuisine, music, dance, artisan crafts, storytelling and cultural demonstrations from 67 nations and ethnic communities — will take place April 10 and 11 at RiverCentre in downtown St. Paul. Tickets are $21 for ages 15 and older, $10 for ages 8-14 and free for children seven and younger. They’re available at ifestmn.org . Founded in 1932 as a street fair, Festival of Nations became a tradition, both for the students bused in on field trips and for the general public. The pandemic forced its cancellation in 2020. It didn’t return in the following years and organizers announced it was permanently shuttered in 2023. Last summer, Twin Cities Jazz Festival founder Steve Heckler announced the return of the annual tradition. He previously served as director from 2005 to 2012. “We’ve had an incredible response,” Heckler said in a news release. “More than 5,000 students from across Minnesota and four additional states are already registered to attend, making this both a powerful educational experience and a vibrant community celebration.” Here’s a gallery of photos from the 2018 Festival of Nations: Kids from the Nasha Shkola Charter School in Brooklyn Park perform at the Festival of Nations at the Saint Paul RiverCentre on Thursday, May 3, 2018. (John Autey / Pioneer Press) Kids from the Nasha Shkola Charter School in Brooklyn Park perform at the Festival of Nations at the Saint Paul RiverCentre on Thursday, May 3, 2018. (John Autey / Pioneer Press) John King from Shakopee blows a Vuvuzela, a horn often used by soccer fans in both South American and South African games, while wearing a Vikings helmet reproduction at the Festival of Nations at the Saint Paul RiverCentre on Thursday, May 3, 2018. (John Autey / Pioneer Press) Lebanese dancers perform at the Festival of Nations at the Saint Paul RiverCentre on Thursday, May 3, 2018. (John Autey / Pioneer Press) A troupe of Greek Dancers perform at the Festival of Nations at the Saint Paul RiverCentre on Thursday, May 3, 2018. (John Autey / Pioneer Press) A volunteer holds up "passports" for...

Outbreak: Shades of Horror Devours XPA with Cross-Platform Multiplayer
content: Category: ID@Xbox March 11, 2026 Outbreak: Shades of Horror Devours XPA with Cross-Platform Multiplayer Julia Wolbach, Co-Founder & Chief Operating Officer, Dead Drop Studios LLC Summary The Spring seasonal event is here. New Raid and map, unlockable cosmetics and more. Story Mode with exciting new maps and gameplay systems. Even the beautiful sight of cherry blossoms and festive masks won’t stop the zombie horde from destroying everything in sight! My name is Julia Wolbach, Co-Founder and Chief Operating Officer of Dead Drop Studios. I’m delighted to introduce you to the Sakura Ridge district just as Outbreak: Shades of Horror joins Xbox Play Anywhere and adds cross-platform multiplayer! No Place to Hide When the Infected Can Strike AnywhereThe many frights and blights of Cypress Ridge can now be experienced whenever and wherever with Xbox Play Anywhere. Looking for a quick four-player co-op online rush with friends? Hop into Raid Mode online on Xbox Live on your Xbox Series X|S console. Have time at work to make a little progress in the tense Chromatic Split: Enhanced Edition campaign? Craft the items you need to survive on handheld with the ROG Xbox Ally X. Prefer to get creative with artsy shots of a city under siege at your desk? Boot into the robust Photo Mode (usable across the whole game) on Windows PC and snap away. With Xbox Play Anywhere, when you purchase Outbreak: Shades of Horror , you can enter the fray on your preferred platform and pick up exactly where you left off with cross-save support. Need more? Cross-platform multiplayer across all versions of the game is now live! The Ruined City of Cypress Ridge Expands with New Sakura Ridge AreaAs part of our major Spring update, players can now explore the scenic Sakura Ridge region in a stunning new map. Lay waste to and dismember legions of zombies as they ravage this once serene locale. Protect civilians for as long as you can by fortifying your defenses, building traps, and shooting the skin off of never-ending waves of enemies in Invasion Mode. Reach the evacuation point in a suspenseful Raid Modescenario where the longer you dawdle, the more difficult and powerful your foes become. Can you distract yourself from the gorgeous scenery long enough to avoid becoming a zombie chew toy? Express Yourself with Unlockable Masks and More!When you load into Cypress Reel, the game’s feature-rich hub world, you’ll see the once-thriving theater has been decorated for the occasion! Take in the added...

Orioles’ home runs beat Pirates
content: SARASOTA, Fla. – Taylor Ward and Jose Barrero each hit a three-run homer, Kyle Bradish pitched five shutout innings and the Baltimore Orioles defeated the Pittsburgh Pirates 7-2 in a Grapefruit League spring training game Wednesday. Ward hit his home run off Pittsburgh starter Noah Davis and it gave the Orioles a 3-0 lead in the fifth inning. Barrero’s home run made it 6-0 in the seventh and came off Pirates reliever Jacob Bimbi. Pittsburgh was held to four hits, including a leadoff homer by Nicholas Cimillo in the ninth inning. It was Cimillo’s second home run of the spring. Bradish allowed one hit and one walk. He struck out two. The Orioles used only two pitchers. Chris Bassitt followed Bradish on the mound and gave up three hits and two runs over four innings. Davis allowed three hits and three runs over 4.2 innings. He walked one and struck out five. Davis pitched in five games for the Los Angeles Dodgers and four with the Minnesota Twins last year. He is trying to make the team as a non-roster invitee to spring training. The Pirates play the Atlanta Braves Thursday evening.

GOACC 2026 Holiday Ornament voting is open
content: OLEAN — It's that time of year again to vote for this year’s Greater Olean Area Chamber of Commerce’s Holiday Ornament is now open, with votes accepted through Monday. This will be the 32nd ornament in a series created by artist Kathleen McDonald. Visit oleanny.com/2026-ornament-voting for the official public ballot. Choose from the list of organizations from previous years’ voting or write in your own. Choices listed this year include YMCA of the Twin Tiers (Olean YMCA), Sprague's Maple Farms, Governor Higgins Mansion, Rock City Park, Bartlett Country Club, The Old Library Restaurant and Oak Hill Park. If the organization that you’d like to vote for is not listed, please select the “Other” choice and fill in your selection.

GOACC announces March Lunch and Learn
content: OLEAN — Ready to move your business onward and upward? Join the Greater Olean Area Chamber of Commerce’s Virtual Lunch & Learn series this month for a presentation from Pursuit, a Buffalo-based business that provides businesses with affordable small business loans and resources so that they can reach higher, transform and grow. On Monday, March 23, at noon, Ethan Ruot will present “Access to Capital for Your Small Business,” a presentation geared toward helping businesses of all kinds, at all stages, achieve and thrive. Pursuit knows you have bold goals and big ideas. Ruot will touch on different loan programs that can help you achieve them. From start-up and working capital loans, to commercial real estate financing, Pursuit has what you need to fund your ambition. To sign up for this Lunch & Learn, visit https://us06web.zoom.us/webinar/register/WN_XNnyANU8T3O650pNAiji_Q#/registration, email Info@OleanNY.com, or call 716-372-4433.

CRCF to host Link and Learn Seminar on fundraising for nonprofits
content: OLEAN — The Cattaraugus Region Community Foundation will host its first Link and Learn Seminar of the year at noon March 25 in The Hub. The session will help nonprofit organizations discover how to perfect their messaging to inspire more and larger donations. With more than two decades of experience in nonprofit leadership, presenter Pamela H. Say also serves as an executive consultant to non-profit organizations in areas such as fundraising, marketing and leadership/management development. Say is also a six-time published author, book coach, developmental editor and ghostwriter. In her work with nonprofit missions, Say has raised millions of dollars, holding roles in stewardship, annual fund management, major gift work and as a C-Suite executive leading entire divisions. Attendees will receive a copy of Say’s book “Five Strategies to Increase Annual Fund Revenue: Groundbreaking Fundraising Solutions for Nonprofits.” “We are excited to bring Pamela Say’s expertise to our nonprofit partners and help them strengthen their impact,” said Kirk Windus, CRCF executive director. “Strong messaging is at the forefront of successful fundraising, and we aim to equip nonprofits with the tools and strategies they need to thrive.” The Hub is located on the fourth floor of the Laine Place Building at 301 N. Union St. in Olean. Individuals can register for the seminar at cattfoundation.org/nonprofit-link-and-learn-series or by contacting Tyler Veno at tyler@cattfoundation.org. The session is free to attend, and lunch and refreshments will be provided free of charge.
